FRANK A. WAMA
CHARLES W. GAYNOR

ROBERT S. SHELBURNE
JAMES H. BOYD, JR.
JAMES E. MESNIER
ROBIN LESLIE STEWART
ROBERT B. MILLER
JAMES H. HARTSHORN
ROBERT A. RUNDSTROM
JAMES E. MESNIER
PAUL A. HASTINGS
PAUL W. TOZER
DONALD W. FITZGERALD
THOMAS C. HUGHES, III
JAMES L. BURKE
RICHARD H. HART, JR
MICHAEL A. GROB
P. ADDISON COVERS
JEFFREY W. BINGHAM
JAN K. DAMESYN
SHERRI MARIE KIRK
DEBORAH J. FICK
ANN M. FREERS

KRONICK, MOSKOVITZ, TIEDEMANN & GIRARD

JAMES P. WIEZEL
ROBERT A. GALGANI
SUSAN B. CARLSEN
PHILIP A. WRIGHT
ALEXANDER W. ROBERTS
ANN SIPRELLE FINAN
MICHAEL McSHANE
GLENN L. LAMOUREUX
LAURIE J. SOPWITH
JANIS J. PURTELL
PAUL C. SMITH
DAVID M. ROSS
HOWARD F. SOSINSKY
JAMES SCOTT YARNELL
MARK L. HEPNER
DIANE M. COLLINS
DONNA M. MATTIES
JEFFREY A. MITCHELL
STEVEN J. MALONE
MARTIN FINE
GEORGE C. HOLLISTER
GRANT C. GOULD
CHRISTOPHER P. GROSSGART
ANDREW B. POLLAK

A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

ATTORNEYS AT LAW
770 L STREET, SUITE 1200
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814-3363
TELEPHONE (916) 444-8920
TELECOPIER (916) 444-5615

August 5, 1992

OF COUNSEL
E. KENDELL DAVIS [illegible]
LEONARD M. FRIEDMAN [illegible]
CHARLES A. BARRETT
MARGARET HASTINGS-HALE

FPPC
AUG 1 - 1992

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Office of the Attorney General
State of California
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Formal Advice

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

In two Advice Letters to the former City Attorney for
the City of Woodland (Moe Advice Letters, A-90-580 and A-90-757),
you advised that two councilmembers, Mayor Elaine Rominger¹ and
Councilmember Joe Crestone, who owned property within 2500 feet
of a proposed development project, could participate in City
Council decisions related to the commencement of a needs
assessment in connection with the proposed project.

Your conclusion was based in large part on an
appraiser's determination that the decisions before the Council
at that time would not have a foreseeable material financial
impact, distinguishable from the public generally, on the
councilmember's properties. You ended Advice Letter A-90-757
with the caution that the advice contained therein "was limited
in its application to the forthcoming decisions concerning the

_________________________
¹    Mayor Rominger was identified in your previous advice
letters as Councilmember Elaine Timothy. The office of mayor in
Woodland is not an elected position; councilmembers rotate
through the mayorship over time. While serving as mayor, a
councilmember has no additional authority beyond her authority as
a councilmember, except to preside over and run the meetings of
the City Council.

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
August 5, 1992
Page 2

9127.1-3

proposed development, and specifically the council vote on
whether to proceed with the needs analysis."

Subsequent to your letters the council decided to
proceed with the needs assessment, which was completed January
21, 1992.² On May 7, 1992, the assessment was presented to the
Woodland Planning Commission for its review. The Planning
Commission voted five to zero, with one commissioner absent and
one disqualifying herself, to accept the needs assessment and to
recommend to the City Council that it decline to proceed further
with the Taormino Project at this time. No action has been taken
by any party with respect to the May 7 decision of the Planning
Commission. Unless the Planning Commission's action is appealed
or otherwise forwarded, the City Council is not required to do
anything in connection with the needs assessment. It is
expected, however, that City Council will be asked to take some
action. Upon review, the Council could decide to do any of the
following:

1) The Council may accept the needs assessment, not
accept the Planning Commission recommendation, and direct staff
to process any applications received from the developer. The
Council is not, however, empowered to act upon the proposed
development at this time.

2) The Council may simply vote to accept the needs
assessment without any directions to staff.

3) The Council may accept the recommendations of the
Planning Commission and direct staff not to process any
applications received from the developer, effectively terminating
the project at this time.

This letter constitutes a formal request for advice
from the FPPC with respect to the duties of Mayor Brommer and
Councilmember Crescione in light of the particular facts set
forth below. This request is made pursuant to Government Code
Section 83114(b) and Title 2 of the California Code of
Regulations, Sections 18320 and 18329.

_________________
2
Relevant excerpts from this document, formally entitled
the "Planning Area M Needs Assessment", are enclosed.

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
August 5, 1992
Page 3

I.  QUESTIONS PRESENTED

    A.  May Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione, each of
whom own real property located within 2500 feet of a proposed
development project, participate in decisions concerning the
project?

    B.  May Mayor Rominger, who has received more than $250
over the past twelve months from the Yolo County Farm Bureau (a
non-profit entity), but who is no longer employed by the entity,
participate in decisions concerning a proposed development
project when:

        1.  The Farm Bureau owns property adjacent to a parcel
of land identified as one of several possible alternative sites
for development of a similarly sized project?

        2.  The Farm Bureau strongly favors preserving
farmland within Yolo County, and the proposed development project
would involve the urbanization of prime agricultural land?

    C.  May the Mayor participate in the review and update of
the City's General Plan, where the General Plan currently
includes policies intended to geographically limit the City's
urban growth and discourage the conversion of agricultural land
to urban uses?

    D.  If the answer to the preceding question is no, may the
Mayor participate in the review and updating of those portions of
the City's General Plan not specifically related to the expansion
of the urbanized portion of the City or the conversion of
agricultural land to urban uses?

II.  FACTS

    A.  Status of the Development Project.

        The proposed development project, known as the
"Taormino Project" or "the Woodlands", calls for development of
275 single family homes and 28 acres of quasi-public facilities
on a 164 acre parcel located outside of both the present
boundaries of the City of the Woodland ("the City") and the

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
August 5, 1992
Page 4

9127.1-3

"urban limit line" identified in the City's 1988 General Plan.3
As we indicated above, the Council will probably be asked to
review the Planning Commission's decision to accept the needs
assessment but recommend against proceeding with the project.

B.    Ownership of Property.

      Mayor Rominger owns her own home at 703 Rubicon Place
Court, within 2,5000 feet of, but more than 300 feet from, the
proposed development.  Councilmember Crescione owns his home at
1614 Donner Way, within 2,500 feet of, but more than 300 feet
from, the proposed development. The enclosed appraisal dated
July 1, 1992, was prepared by an independent appraiser hired by
the City to evaluate the potential impact on the value of these
two properties of the decisions currently before the Council
concerning the Taormino Project.

C.    Mayor Rominger's Former Employment.

      We have also enclosed a copy of a letter (with
attachments) dated June 5, 1992, from Mr. Alexander Froehlich, a
resident of the City.  In addition to the question of whether
Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione have a conflict of
interest because of the property each owns, Mr. Froehlich raises
concerns based on the relationship between Mayor Rominger and the
Yolo County Farm Bureau ("the Farm Bureau").

      For purposes of this letter only, and except as
otherwise noted below, we accept as substantially accurate the
factual statements contained within Mr. Froehlich's letter.
Although we endorse neither Mr. Froehlich's legal analysis nor
his conclusions, we have included the entire letter in the belief
that more information is better.

      1.    Mayor Rominger's previous employment
            by the Farm Bureau and her duties.

      The Yolo County Farm Bureau is a California non-
profit mutual benefit corporation and a member of the California
Farm Bureau.  Mayor Rominger was the Secretary-Treasurer and
Manager of the Farm Bureau until she resigned those positions

___________________
3     Defined as "[a] line beyond which urban development is
not planned or permitted.  An urban limit line does not
necessarily coincide with the boundaries of the General Plan
Area." (Woodland General Plan--Policy Plan, p. 15.)

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel                          9127.1-3
August 5, 1992
Page 5


effective July 15, 1992.⁴ The duties of the Secretary and
Treasurer are detailed in Article IX, §§6 and 7 of the Farm
Bureau by-laws, a copy of which is attached to Mr. Froehlich's
letter.

     As Manager, Mayor Rominger received income of more than
$250 per year,⁵ and was responsible for the day-to-day operation
of the Farm Bureau. Contrary to Mr. Froehlich's suggestion, her
duties as Manager and Secretary-Treasurer did not involve the
formulation of policy or Farm Bureau positions on specific
issues,⁶ although she did occasionally appear before the Yolo
County Board of Supervisors as an advocate of the policies and
positions adopted by the Farm Bureau.⁷ The land use policies
advocated by the Farm Bureau include, as a general statement,
opposition to the urbanization of prime agricultural lands within
the County. The Farm Bureau rarely takes a position on issues
under consideration by the Woodland Planning Commission or City
Council, and has not taken a formal position on the Taormino
Project.

     2.    The Farm Bureau's real property.

     The Farm Bureau is a non-profit entity with gross
annual receipts of approximately $200,000. It's principal asset
is a 8.34 acre parcel of real property located within the City of
Woodland. As required by the General Plan, the needs assessment
identified and evaluated alternative sites where a large lot

_____________________

     ⁴ Mayor Rominger was first elected to the Woodland City
Council in 1990. She became a paid employee of the Farm Bureau
in 1986, four years before she was first elected to the City
Council. She became Manager of the Farm Bureau in 1987, three
years before her election to the City Council.

     ⁵ As Mr. Froehlich notes in his letter, Mayor Rominger's
husband is the President of the Farm Bureau and on its Board of
Directors. These positions are unpaid, however, and consequently
should not constitute a "source of income" to Mayor Rominger.

     ⁶ Farm Bureau policies and positions are determined by
the Board of Directors and the voting members of the Farm Bureau.
Mayor Rominger is not now a member of the Board of Directors.

     ⁷ Although the Yolo County Farm Bureau has not adopted
its own County land use policies, it does advocate those land use
policies adopted by the California Farm Bureau.

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
August 5, 1992
Page 6

9127.1-3

development on the scale proposed by the developer of the Paolomino Project could be located. The Farm Bureau property is
adjacent to one of several parcels discussed in the "alternative
locations" portion of the needs assessment (the "Aoki parcel").
(Needs assessment, pp. 50-51.) The Farm Bureau property itself
was not considered and is not the subject of any study or action.

In view of the fact that Mayor Rominger no longer
receives income from the Farm Bureau, 2 Cal. Code Regs.
Section 18702.5 appears inapplicable. In any event, there does
not appear to be a "substantial likelihood" that any of the
possible Council decisions discussed above will affect the value
of the Farm Bureau property by $50,000 or more.

D.  Review of the City's General Plan.

The City's 1988 General Plan establishes certain
policies concerning development within the City's current
boundaries and expansion of those boundaries. These policies,
developed in conjunction with Yolo County, seek to maintain the
farm-oriented character of the City, to provide for orderly
growth, to ensure that adequate levels of service will be
maintained, and to limit the conversion of prime agricultural
land to urban uses. These policies are implemented through the
use of phased growth areas and an Urban Limit Line (defined in
footnote 3 above).

The City Council is currently considering whether to
begin the process of updating the General Plan, a project which
it is estimated will take up to two years. A full update of the
General Plan will necessarily include review of the policies
described above. The Council is not currently obligated to
commence a full update.

F.  The July 1992 Independent Appraisal.

The July 1, 1992 appraisal performed by Lynch &
Associates concluded that upon completion of the Paolomino
project, "[n]o value increase will be realized from the project
by either council members homes. Due to increased supply of
homes a slight decrease may be realized in the 2 to 3 years of
under $500." Accordingly, the conclusion reached in Advice
Letter A-90-757 still appears applicable, and neither Mayor
Rominger nor Councilmember Crescione should be required to
refrain from participating in Council decisions pursuant to
2 Cal. Code Regs. Section 18702.3(a)(3).

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
August 5, 1992
Page 7

9127.1-3

We hope that the information provided in this letter
and the enclosures hereto will be sufficient, but if you have any
further questions concerning this matter, please do not hesitate
to call me.

Sincerely,

KRONICK, MOSKOWITZ, TIEDEMANN & GIRARD

ROBERT E. MURPHY
City Attorney, City of Woodland

63491.2
Enclosures
cc: Mayor Elaine Rominger
Councilmember Joe Crescione
Kris Kristensen, City Manager
Alexander Froehlich

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione can participate in City Council decisions regarding the Taormino development project given their property ownership within 2500 feet of the project, and whether Mayor Rominger has disqualifying conflicts based on her former employment relationship with the Yolo County Farm Bureau and participation in General Plan review.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an incoming formal advice request TO the FPPC (not a response FROM the FPPC). The document contains detailed Questions Presented and Facts sections but no Conclusion section, as it is seeking FPPC guidance. Document references prior FPPC advice letters A-90-580 and A-90-757. The sender is requesting advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A. May Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione, each of whom own real property located within 2500 feet of a proposed development project, participate in decisions concerning the project?

B. May Mayor Rominger, who has received more than $250 over the past twelve months from the Yolo County Farm Bureau (a non-profit entity), but who is no longer employed by the entity, participate in decisions concerning a proposed development project when:
1. The Farm Bureau owns property adjacent to a parcel of land identified as one of several possible alternative sites for development of a similarly sized project?
2. The Farm Bureau strongly favors preserving farmland within Yolo County, and the proposed development project would involve the urbanization of prime agricultural land?

C. May the Mayor participate in the review and update of the City's General Plan, where the General Plan currently includes policies intended to geographically limit the City's urban growth and discourage the conversion of agricultural land to urban uses?

D. If the answer to the preceding question is no, may the Mayor participate in the review and updating of those portions of the City's General Plan not specifically related to the expansion of the urbanized portion of the City or the conversion of agricultural land to urban uses?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request for formal FPPC advice from the Acting General Counsel regarding conflict of interest issues for Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione in Woodland City Council decisions on a proposed 275-unit development project (Taormino Project), including concerns about property ownership within 2500 feet and Mayor Rominger's former employment with the Yolo County Farm Bureau.
